1.synthesis:
Moses, 120 years old and informed by God that he will not cross the Jordan, gathers all Israel for his final administrative acts: commissioning Joshua as his successor, handing over the written law to the Levitical priests, and establishing the septennial public reading of the law at the Feast of Tabernacles. His message to the people is threefold encouragement: "Be strong and courageous. Do not fear or be in dread of them, for it is the LORD your God who goes with you. He will not leave you or forsake you" (v. 6). To Joshua he gives the same charge: "Be strong and courageous, for you shall go with this people into the land... The LORD is the one who goes before you. He will be with you; he will not leave you or forsake you. Do not fear or be dismayed" (vv. 7-8).

Matthew Henry observes that Moses, though barred from Canaan himself, pours his energy into preparing those who will enter. His generosity toward Joshua — encouraging the people to follow him publicly — is noted: "Moses does not go out with a bad grace, or like one forced from his place, but with all the dignity and decency imaginable." Keil and Delitzsch describe the law-depositing with the priests (v. 9) and the septennial reading (vv. 10-13) as completing Moses's legislative work: he had spoken the law; now he writes it, hands it over, and institutionalizes its public hearing so that future generations will know it.
1.historical_context: The septennial reading of the law (vv. 10-13) was to occur "at the end of every seven years, at the set time in the year of release, at the Feast of Tabernacles" — linking the public Torah reading to the sabbatical year. Adam Clarke explains the practical significance: in an oral culture with limited literacy, the public reading before "men, women, children, and the sojourner within your towns" was the primary mechanism for Torah transmission across generations. The phrase "that their children, who have not known it, may hear and learn" (v. 13) acknowledges that covenant knowledge cannot be assumed to transmit automatically — it requires intentional, institutionalized teaching.
1.literary_structure: Verses 1-8 form the public commissioning: Moses's announcement of his own incapacity (v. 2), assurance of divine provision through Joshua (vv. 3-5), charge to the people (v. 6), charge to Joshua (vv. 7-8). Verses 9-13 record the law's deposit with the priests (v. 9) and the covenant for septennial public reading (vv. 10-13). The passage thus addresses both leadership succession (Joshua) and institutional Torah transmission (priests and reading), the two pillars needed to sustain covenant faithfulness after Moses.
1.theological_application: Matthew Henry extracts from verse 6 ("he will not leave you or forsake you") a universal principle: "There is no end of the ways and means by which God will provide for his people's support and encouragement in this world." Calvin observes that Moses's willingness to hand authority to Joshua without bitterness or jealousy is itself a mark of genuine piety: "A man truly animated by the divine Spirit is not stirred by personal ambition." The septennial Torah reading serves the same purpose as the law on doorposts (Deut 6:9) and in the heart (Deut 30:14): God's word is to permeate every dimension of Israel's life so that forgetting becomes structurally difficult.
1.christology: "He will not leave you or forsake you" (v. 6) is quoted in Hebrews 13:5 as a promise applicable to every believer: "He has said, 'I will never leave you nor forsake you.'" The promise originally given to Israel facing Canaan is applied to Christians facing any trial, because it is ultimately a promise about God's character — and in Christ, the incarnate God has pledged his permanent presence: "And behold, I am with you always, to the end of the age" (Matt 28:20). Joshua (the same name as Jesus, both meaning "the LORD saves") who leads the people into rest is a type of Christ our Joshua, who leads his people into the "rest" that the earthly Canaan only symbolized (Heb 4:8-9).

14.synthesis:
God summons Moses and Joshua to the tent of meeting, where he appears in a pillar of cloud — the last time the Shekinah appears to Moses at the tabernacle. There the LORD tells Moses plainly: after his death, Israel will prostitute themselves to foreign gods, break the covenant, and face the disasters listed in Deuteronomy 28 (vv. 16-17). In that day they will say, "Have not these evils come upon us because our God is not among us?" — yet will not acknowledge their own role. God therefore directs Moses to write a song and teach it to Israel, so that it will serve as a permanent witness "against them" when the disasters come (vv. 19-21). The song will not be forgotten from the mouths of their descendants, even in exile, and will function as a divine testimony.

Matthew Henry identifies the dual purpose of the song: as prevention ("this song, if rightly improved, might be a means to prevent their apostasy") and as recovery ("if this song did not prevent their apostasy, yet it might help to bring them to repentance"). Calvin observes that Moses's willingness to teach a song that indicted his own people — that rendered "their name detestable" — was itself a mark of his prophetic courage: "He did not fear to irritate all their minds... had not the secret inspiration of the Spirit interposed to subdue them."
14.historical_context: The commissioning of a prophetic song as a covenant witness is without parallel in the ancient Near East. Ellicott notes that the last recorded appearance of God in a pillar of cloud at the tabernacle is here (v. 15), marking the formal close of the Mosaic era of visible divine manifestation. The song of chapter 32 was designed for a culture of oral transmission — it would be sung and remembered when written texts might be lost. Adam Clarke's structural comment is apt: the song functions as a mnemonic sermon, its poetic form ensuring memorability even through generations of apostasy.
14.literary_structure: Verses 14-15 record the divine summons to the tabernacle and the theophany. Verses 16-22 contain God's prediction of apostasy and the command to write the song. Verses 23-29 record Joshua's commissioning (v. 23), the completion of the law-writing (vv. 24-27), its deposit beside the ark (v. 26), and Moses's final prediction of post-death rebellion (vv. 27-29). The section functions as a theological bridge between the legislative work of Deuteronomy and the prophetic song of chapter 32.
14.theological_application: Matthew Henry draws the pastoral lesson that God gives warning before judgment — the song is a "medicine prepared beforehand for their cure." God's foreknowledge of Israel's apostasy does not relieve Israel of responsibility; rather, it makes the warning more urgent. The phrase "their imaginations that they are already imagining today, before I have brought them into the land" (v. 21) indicates that the seeds of future apostasy are already present — and God, who knows every imagination, provides a specific counter-word. Gill observes that placing the law "beside the ark" (v. 26) rather than in the ark (where the tablets were, Deut 10:2) may suggest its role as external testimony against the people, while the tablets within the ark represent the inner covenant obligations.
14.christology: The song of Moses is called in Revelation 15:3 "the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb" — the two songs are sung together by the redeemed standing on the sea of glass. This identification suggests that the song of Moses, which witnesses to God's righteousness in judgment and salvation, finds its ultimate expression in the song celebrating Christ's redemption. Calvin's observation that Moses taught a song indicting his people by divine compulsion finds its antitype in the cross, where Christ bore the testimony of the law's witness against sinners in order to satisfy it — so that those who once heard the law's song of accusation now sing the Lamb's song of redemption.
